The client persists the start response and replays the identifying fields
here; the server reconstructs the same staging key, asks S3 which parts it
already holds, and re-presigns fresh URLs for the remaining parts.

BuildMultipartUploadResumeResponse.description:
Response that lets a client finish an interrupted multipart upload.

parts re-presigns every part (URLs from the original start may have
expired); uploaded_parts tells the client which of those to skip and
carries the ETags it needs to assemble the object at complete_url.

part_size.description: Size in bytes of every part except the last

upload_expires_at.title: Upload Expires At

uploaded_parts.title: Uploaded Parts

file_name.description: Name of the file to upload
file_name.title: File Name

file_size.title: File Size

BuildMultipartUploadStartRequest.title: BuildMultipartUploadStartRequest

BuildMultipartUploadStartResponse.description:
Response with everything a client needs to upload parts directly to S3.

Like upload-session, no build row exists yet: the client uploads the
parts, assembles them by POSTing the part ETags to complete_url, then
creates the build via POST /{app_id}/builds with upload_id. Unfinished
multipart uploads are aborted by the bucket lifecycle rule and abandoned
staging objects are swept server-side; abort_url is a courtesy for
prompt cleanup.

part_size.description: Size in bytes of every part except the last
part_size.title: Part Size

s3_upload_id.description: Native S3 multipart UploadId. Already embedded in every presigned URL above; surfaced explicitly so a client can persist it and resume an interrupted upload via the multipart-upload/resume route.
s3_upload_id.title: S3 Upload Id

upload_expires_at.description: Unix timestamp after which the presigned URLs expire
upload_expires_at.title: Upload Expires At

upload_id.description: Staging upload session id, passed to POST /{app_id}/builds
upload_id.title: Upload Id

post.description:
Create a build from a completed staging upload.

Locates the artifact staged by the upload session, validates it
server-side, copies it to its permanent location, and inserts the build
row. Invalid artifacts never produce a build row; their staging objects
are deleted immediately.

Create a build by fetching an artifact from a protected URL (e.g., EAS).

Behavior:
- Validates org ownership and mobile platform (service accounts bypass org check)
- Idempotent on (app_id, version): returns existing row if present
- Streams download, computes sha256, uploads to S3 under org prefix
- For tar/tar.gz files: extracts .app directory before uploading
- Persists builds row with artifact_url and merged metadata

Resume an interrupted multipart upload started by the route above.

S3 is the only durable record of an in-progress upload (no build row
exists yet), so this asks S3 which parts it already holds and re-presigns
fresh URLs for every part. The client uploads only the missing parts and
assembles the object with the returned ETags — a dropped connection costs
one part, never the whole artifact. The part_size is recomputed from the
unchanged file_size, so it matches the original start deterministically.

Start an S3 multipart upload into the staging area.

Like upload-session, no build row is created here: the client uploads
parts directly to S3, assembles them with the presigned complete URL,
then POST /{app_id}/builds validates the staged artifact and inserts the
build row — identical to the single-PUT staging flow from that point on.
The abort URL is a courtesy: the bucket's AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload
lifecycle rule frees unfinished parts, and assembled-but-never-finalized
staging objects are swept when a later session for the same app starts.

post.description:
Stop or cancel a device session.

Called by ``revyl device stop`` and the frontend stop button. Despite
the endpoint name, this handles both true cancellations (test was
interrupted) and normal stops (bare device session ended by user).

The final status depends on session type:
  - **Bare sessions** (``is_simulation=True``, no ``test_id``): these
    have no ``test_executions`` row.  Stopping is a normal completion,
    so the status is set to ``"completed"``.
  - **Test sessions** (started from test editor, ``revyl create``, etc.):
    these have a ``test_executions`` row.  Stopping interrupts the test,
    so the status is ``"cancelled"`` with an error message.

After updating the DB, a Hatchet workflow cancel is attempted
(best-effort).  Hatchet failures are logged and surfaced in the
response but do not cause the endpoint to return an error.

Args:
    workflow_run_id: The Hatchet workflow run ID to stop/cancel.
    auth_info: Authenticated caller (injected by Depends).

Returns:
    JSON dict with ``success``, ``db_updated``, and ``hatchet_cancelled``.
